name: Warburg effect and cancer metabolic rewiring
summary: The reprogrammed metabolism of proliferating tumor cells: high glucose uptake with aerobic lactate secretion (the Warburg effect), suppression of pyruvate oxidation, glutamine anaplerosis and biosynthetic diversions, orchestrated by HIF-1α, MYC and loss of p53.
description: Otto Warburg observed that tumors consume glucose avidly and secrete lactate even when oxygen is plentiful ('aerobic glycolysis'). Cancer cells achieve this by up-regulating the glucose transporter GLUT1 and expressing high-flux, less product-inhibited isoenzymes — hexokinase 2 (HK2) docked on the mitochondrial outer membrane, the bifunctional kinase PFKFB3 that raises the allosteric activator fructose-2,6-bisphosphate, the low-activity M2 isoform of pyruvate kinase (PKM2), and lactate dehydrogenase A (LDHA). PKM2's restrained activity backs glycolytic intermediates up into anabolic branches (pentose-phosphate pathway for NADPH/ribose, and the serine synthesis pathway via PHGDH). Pyruvate is diverted from the mitochondrial pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDH) to lactate: HIF-1α induces pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase 1 (PDK1), which phosphorylates and inactivates PDH, while LDHA regenerates the NAD+ needed to sustain glycolysis, and the monocarboxylate transporter MCT4 exports lactate and protons to acidify the microenvironment. In parallel, tumors are 'glutamine-addicted': ASCT2 imports glutamine, glutaminase (GLS) and glutamate dehydrogenase (GLUD1) feed carbon into the TCA cycle as α-ketoglutarate (anaplerosis) and supply nitrogen for biosynthesis. This rewiring is driven by oncogenic signaling (PI3K–AKT–mTOR), by the transcription factors HIF-1α and MYC, and by loss of the tumor suppressor p53 (whose target TIGAR normally restrains glycolysis by lowering fructose-2,6-bisphosphate). This module captures the reactions and control edges that distinguish cancer central-carbon metabolism from normal glycolysis; the core glycolytic backbone itself is documented in the 'glycolysis' module.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 14 & Box 14-1

references.title: Understanding the Warburg effect: the metabolic requirements of cell proliferation
The Emerging Hallmarks of Cancer Metabolism
Hypoxia-inducible factors in physiology and medicine
The M2 splice isoform of pyruvate kinase is important for cancer metabolism and tumour growth
Pyruvate kinase M2 activators promote tetramer formation and suppress tumorigenesis
Serine is a natural ligand and allosteric activator of pyruvate kinase M2
HIF-1-mediated expression of pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase: a metabolic switch required for cellular adaptation to hypoxia
The plasma membrane lactate transporter MCT4, but not MCT1, is up-regulated by hypoxia through a HIF-1alpha-dependent mechanism
c-Myc suppression of miR-23a/b enhances mitochondrial glutaminase expression and glutamine metabolism
TIGAR, a p53-inducible regulator of glycolysis and apoptosis
The tumor suppressor p53 down-regulates glucose transporters GLUT1 and GLUT4 gene expression
Phosphoglycerate dehydrogenase diverts glycolytic flux and contributes to oncogenesis
Functional genomics reveal that the serine synthesis pathway is essential in breast cancer

compartments.name: Extracellular space / tumor microenvironment
compartments.description: Source of imported glucose and glutamine; sink for secreted lactate and protons.

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Site of glycolysis, lactate production and the serine-synthesis branch point.

compartments.name: Mitochondrion (matrix)
compartments.description: Site of PDH, glutaminase and glutamate dehydrogenase; TCA-cycle anaplerosis.

compartments.name: Plasma membrane
compartments.description: Location of GLUT1, MCT4 and ASCT2 transporters.

compartments.name: Nucleus
compartments.description: Site of HIF-1α / MYC / p53 transcriptional control of the metabolic program.

metabolites.name: D-Glucose
metabolites.description: Primary carbon fuel taken up at high rate by tumor cells.

metabolites.name: D-Glucose 6-phosphate
metabolites.description: First committed metabolite; branch point into the oxidative pentose-phosphate pathway.

metabolites.name: D-Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate
metabolites.description: Glycolytic intermediate and the endogenous allosteric activator that promotes the active PKM2 tetramer.

metabolites.name: D-Fructose 2,6-bisphosphate
metabolites.description: Most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1; its level (raised by PFKFB3, lowered by TIGAR) sets glycolytic flux.

metabolites.name: Pyruvate
metabolites.description: Node whose fate (lactate vs acetyl-CoA) defines the Warburg switch.

metabolites.name: L-Lactate
metabolites.description: End product secreted by glycolytic tumor cells; acidifies the microenvironment and can fuel neighboring oxidative cells (metabolic symbiosis).

metabolites.name: Acetyl-CoA
metabolites.description: Product of PDH; entry point of pyruvate carbon into the TCA cycle, largely bypassed in aerobic glycolysis.

metabolites.name: L-Glutamine
metabolites.description: Second major fuel of proliferating tumor cells; carbon and nitrogen source.

metabolites.name: L-Glutamate
metabolites.description: Product of glutaminolysis; nitrogen donor and precursor of α-ketoglutarate and glutathione.

metabolites.name: 2-Oxoglutarate (α-ketoglutarate)
metabolites.description: Anaplerotic entry of glutamine carbon into the TCA cycle.

metabolites.name: 3-Phospho-D-glycerate
metabolites.description: Glycolytic intermediate diverted into the serine-synthesis pathway by PHGDH in many cancers.

metabolites.name: 3-Phosphohydroxypyruvate
metabolites.description: First committed intermediate of de novo serine synthesis.

metabolites.name: L-Serine
metabolites.description: Biosynthetic product and allosteric activator of PKM2, coupling serine availability to glycolytic flux.

metabolites.name: NADH
metabolites.description: Reoxidized to NAD+ by LDHA, sustaining high glycolytic flux without mitochondrial reoxidation.

metabolites.name: Proton (H+)
metabolites.description: Co-exported with lactate by MCT4, contributing to extracellular acidosis.

enzymes.name: Facilitated glucose transporter member 1 (GLUT1)
enzymes.description: Passive glucose uniporter; over-expressed in most tumors and a canonical HIF-1α/MYC target and p53-repressed gene.

enzymes.name: Hexokinase 2
enzymes.description: High-affinity hexokinase that docks on the mitochondrial outer membrane (VDAC), coupling ATP from oxidative phosphorylation to glucose phosphorylation and resisting product (G6P) inhibition; the dominant hexokinase isoform in cancer.

enzymes.name: 6-Phosphofructo-2-kinase/fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase 3 (PFKFB3)
enzymes.description: Bifunctional enzyme whose kinase activity dominates (very high kinase:bisphosphatase ratio), producing fructose-2,6-bisphosphate — the most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1. Induced in hypoxia and proliferating cells.

enzymes.name: Pyruvate kinase M2 (PKM2)
enzymes.description: Embryonic splice isoform of pyruvate kinase (same PKM gene as PKM1) preferentially expressed in tumors. Its allosterically tunable, often low activity lets upstream glycolytic intermediates accumulate for biosynthesis; activity is raised by fructose-1,6-bisphosphate and serine and lowered by growth-factor tyrosine phosphorylation.

enzymes.name: L-Lactate dehydrogenase A chain (LDHA)
enzymes.description: Reduces pyruvate to lactate while regenerating NAD+; the enzyme most directly responsible for aerobic lactate production, a HIF-1α and MYC target.

enzymes.name: Monocarboxylate transporter 4 (MCT4)
enzymes.description: Low-affinity, high-capacity proton-coupled exporter of lactate; strongly HIF-1α-inducible and adapted to relieve glycolytic cells of their lactic-acid load.

enzymes.name: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex, E1 component α (PDH)
enzymes.description: Gateway of pyruvate carbon into the TCA cycle. Its E1 subunit is inactivated by PDK-mediated serine phosphorylation, the switch that diverts pyruvate to lactate in the Warburg state.

enzymes.name: Pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase 1 (PDK1)
enzymes.description: HIF-1α-inducible kinase that phosphorylates and inhibits the PDH complex, actively down-regulating mitochondrial pyruvate oxidation.

enzymes.name: Neutral amino acid transporter B(0) / ASCT2
enzymes.description: Sodium-dependent obligatory exchanger that provides the principal route of glutamine uptake in glutamine-addicted tumor cells; a MYC-supported target.

enzymes.name: Glutaminase, kidney-type (GLS)
enzymes.description: Hydrolyzes glutamine to glutamate, the committed step of glutaminolysis; expression is raised in cancer downstream of MYC (via repression of miR-23a/b).

enzymes.name: Glutamate dehydrogenase 1 (GLUD1)
enzymes.description: NAD(P)+-dependent oxidative deamination of glutamate to α-ketoglutarate, feeding glutamine carbon into the TCA cycle (anaplerosis) and releasing ammonia.

enzymes.name: D-3-Phosphoglycerate dehydrogenase (PHGDH)
enzymes.description: Committed, often amplified enzyme that branches 3-phosphoglycerate off glycolysis into serine and one-carbon metabolism to support nucleotide and glutathione synthesis.

enzymes.name: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase TIGAR
enzymes.description: p53-inducible enzyme (TP53-induced glycolysis and apoptosis regulator) that lowers fructose-2,6-bisphosphate, dampening glycolysis and shunting glucose toward the pentose-phosphate pathway; loss of p53 removes this brake.

enzymes.name: Hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha (HIF-1α)
enzymes.description: Oxygen-labile transcription factor stabilized by hypoxia and by oncogenic signaling; the master activator of the glycolytic gene program (GLUT1, HK2, PFKFB3, LDHA, PDK1, MCT4). Modeled here as a protein regulator.

enzymes.name: MYC proto-oncogene protein (c-Myc)
enzymes.description: Oncogenic transcription factor that coordinately up-regulates glucose and glutamine metabolism (GLUT1, LDHA, PKM splicing toward M2, and glutaminase via miR-23 repression). Modeled here as a protein regulator.

enzymes.name: Cellular tumor antigen p53
enzymes.description: Tumor suppressor that opposes the Warburg phenotype by repressing glucose transporters and inducing TIGAR (and SCO2); its frequent loss in cancer releases these restraints. Modeled here as a protein regulator.

genes.name: Solute carrier family 2 member 1 (GLUT1)
genes.description: Encodes the GLUT1 glucose transporter; HIF-1α/MYC-induced, p53-repressed.

genes.name: Lactate dehydrogenase A
genes.description: HIF-1α and MYC target encoding LDHA.

genes.name: Pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase 1
genes.description: HIF-1α target; product inactivates the PDH complex.

genes.name: Glutaminase (kidney-type)
genes.description: Its expression is raised by MYC through repression of miR-23a/b.

genes.name: Pyruvate kinase M1/M2
genes.description: Alternative splicing (favored in cancer) yields the M2 isoform PKM2.

genes.name: TP53-induced glycolysis and apoptosis regulator
genes.description: p53-inducible fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase gene (also known as C12orf5).

provenance.notes: Passive facilitative uniport driven by the inward glucose gradient; markedly elevated flux underlies tumor [18F]-FDG-PET avidity.
HK2 traps glucose as G6P; mitochondrial binding gives it privileged access to ATP and reduced sensitivity to G6P feedback.

Regulatory, not mass-throughput: F2,6BP is a signaling metabolite that allosterically activates PFK-1 to accelerate glycolysis. The reverse (phosphatase) reaction is a separate activity (see TIGAR, war12).
Catalyzed in tumors by the M2 isoform, whose tunable low activity creates a bottleneck that backs intermediates up into anabolic branches.

reactions.name: Pyruvate → lactate (lactate dehydrogenase A) — Warburg hallmark

provenance.notes: Thermodynamically reversible but driven toward lactate in tumors; regenerates cytosolic NAD+ to sustain GAPDH and high glycolytic flux even under normoxia (aerobic glycolysis).
Electroneutral lactate:H+ symport; net efflux acidifies the tumor microenvironment and unloads the glycolytic acid burden.

reactions.name: Pyruvate oxidation to acetyl-CoA (PDH complex) — suppressed in Warburg
reactions.equation: Pyruvate + CoA + NAD+ -> Acetyl-CoA + CO2 + NADH

provenance.notes: The alternative, oxidative fate of pyruvate (after mitochondrial pyruvate-carrier import). Actively down-regulated in the Warburg state by HIF-1α-induced PDK1 phosphorylation of the E1 subunit (see reg edges). EC 1.2.4.1 denotes the E1 decarboxylating component of the multienzyme complex.
Diversion of glycolytic 3-PG into serine/one-carbon metabolism; PHGDH is recurrently amplified (melanoma, breast cancer) and rate-limiting for this branch.
ASCT2/SLC1A5 is a Na+-dependent obligate antiporter; net glutamine import shown here. Glutamine addiction is a hallmark of many proliferating tumors.
Committed step of glutaminolysis; GLS is up-regulated downstream of MYC.

reactions.name: Glutamate → α-ketoglutarate (glutamate dehydrogenase, anaplerosis)
reactions.equation: L-Glutamate + H2O + NAD+ -> 2-Oxoglutarate + Ammonia + NADH + 2 H+

provenance.notes: GLUD1 is NAD(P)+-dependent (EC 1.4.1.3); the NAD+-linked equation and KEGG R00243 are shown. Confidence set to medium because in many tumors the dominant route from glutamate to α-ketoglutarate is transaminase-mediated (GOT2/GPT2) rather than oxidative deamination — see unverified notes.

reactions.name: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate hydrolysis (TIGAR) — p53 brake on glycolysis

provenance.notes: p53-induced fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase that lowers F2,6BP, opposing PFKFB3 and dampening glycolysis while promoting PPP flux. Confidence medium: TIGAR's preferred physiological substrate (F2,6BP vs 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate) remains debated.

regulations.mechanism: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate binds PKM2 and promotes the high-activity tetramer (feedforward coupling of upstream glycolysis to pyruvate-kinase flux); a distinct binding site from synthetic activators.

Serine is a natural allosteric activator of PKM2; when serine is abundant PKM2 is active, and serine deprivation lowers PKM2 activity to redirect glucose carbon into serine biosynthesis — a rheostat linking one-carbon supply to glycolytic flux.

Product (glucose-6-phosphate) feedback inhibition of hexokinase; HK2 is comparatively resistant and its docking on mitochondria further relieves this inhibition, enabling sustained high glucose phosphorylation in tumors.
PDK1 phosphorylates serine residues on the PDH E1α subunit (PDHA1), inactivating the complex and blocking pyruvate entry into the TCA cycle — the covalent switch that shunts pyruvate to lactate.
Acetyl-CoA product feedback inhibits the PDH complex (via the E2 transacetylase and by activating PDK), reinforcing suppression of pyruvate oxidation.
A high NADH/NAD+ ratio product-inhibits PDH (and stimulates PDK), coupling mitochondrial redox state to down-regulation of pyruvate oxidation.
HIF-1α binds hypoxia-response elements in the SLC2A1 promoter to induce the GLUT1 glucose transporter, raising glucose uptake.
HIF-1α transactivates HK2, coordinately raising glucose-phosphorylating capacity with uptake.
HIF-1α induces PFKFB3, raising fructose-2,6-bisphosphate and thereby PFK-1 activity and glycolytic flux.
LDHA is a classic HIF-1α target; its induction maximizes conversion of pyruvate to lactate with NAD+ regeneration.
HIF-1α directly transactivates PDK1; the resulting PDH inhibition down-regulates mitochondrial oxygen consumption and diverts pyruvate to lactate.
HIF-1α binds hypoxia-response elements in the MCT4 (SLC16A3) promoter — but not MCT1 — inducing the lactate/H+ exporter to clear the acid load of aerobic glycolysis.
MYC transactivates LDHA as part of its glycolytic program, complementing HIF-1α and enforcing lactate production.
MYC transcriptionally represses miR-23a/b, derepressing (raising) mitochondrial glutaminase (GLS) protein and driving glutamine catabolism — an indirect but robust induction.
MYC contributes to GLUT1 induction and to broad up-regulation of glycolytic genes, cooperating with HIF-1α to raise glucose uptake.
MYC raises PKM expression and, via induction of the splicing factors hnRNPA1/A2 and PTB, biases alternative splicing toward the M2 (PKM2) isoform favored by tumors.
Wild-type p53 binds and represses the GLUT1 (and GLUT4) promoter, restraining glucose uptake; DNA-binding-domain mutations common in cancer abolish this repression, raising glucose metabolism.
p53 induces TIGAR, whose fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase activity lowers F2,6BP to dampen glycolysis and lower ROS; loss of p53 removes this glycolytic brake, favoring the Warburg phenotype.

relations.note: LDHA gene encodes lactate dehydrogenase A.
PDK1 gene encodes pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase 1.
SLC16A3 encodes the MCT4 lactate/H+ transporter.
The PKM gene encodes pyruvate kinase; alternative splicing yields the M2 isoform PKM2.
TIGAR gene encodes the p53-inducible fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase.
PFKFB3-derived fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is the most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1, accelerating flux through the glycolysis module (opposed by p53/TIGAR).
Glucose-6-phosphate is diverted into the oxidative PPP for NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate; PKM2's bottleneck and TIGAR both promote this shunt to support proliferation and redox balance.
PHGDH branches 3-phosphoglycerate into serine and one-carbon metabolism, supplying nucleotides, glutathione and methylation capacity.
Pyruvate's oxidative fate via PDH feeds the TCA cycle; this route is actively suppressed by PDK1 in the Warburg state.
Glutamine-derived α-ketoglutarate replenishes TCA-cycle carbon (anaplerosis), the mainstay of glutamine addiction.
Tumor glutaminolysis draws on the systemic glutamine pool detailed in the glutamate–glutamine module.
Lactate exported by tumors can be recycled to glucose in the liver (Cori cycle), a systemic feature of cancer cachexia.
